Thanks for spending time on this, I'm happy to help you get this set up!

Mode was split out from a project that we're going to open source soon, and it uses it extensively.
There's a lot of hooks and stuff in mode that lets you extend it, but hardly any of it is documented. Most of the time spent documenting anything is spent on this other project, but hope to document more of mode soon.

FIrst of all you should probably use `mode.utils.logging.get_logger`, as it performs the best practice of
adding a NullHandler:

```py
# proj/__init__.py
from mode.utils.logging import get_logger

logger = get_logger(__name__)
```

setting the level you could do, but I'm not sure it's necessary.


You don't have to define `on_setup_root_logger` at all,  as the Worker will set up the root logger for you based on the `loglevel` and `logfile` arguments.

What we do is that we have one logger for every module:

```
# `proj/services/discovery.py`
logger = get_logger(__name__)
```


These all propagate to the root logger, so the Worker only needs to configure that one.

Our project also has an `App` class, but it does not inherit from the worker.
In our case the "app" is something the user defines, kind of like in Celery:

```
my_app = Celery()
```

The app is a service that when started starts other services:

```
from mode import Service
from mode.utils.objects import cached_property

class App(Service):

    async def on_start(self):
       await self.add_runtime_dependency(self.consumer)
       await self.add_runtime_dependency(self.producer)

   @cached_property
   def consumer(self) -> Consumer:
        return Consumer(app=self)

    @cached_property
    def producer(self) -> Producer:
        Producer(app=self)
```

One specific issue with our App class is that users will define them at module scope.
Instantiating a service at module scope is bad, because it creates asyncio.Event's that require the event loop to be created.
To fix that we use mode.proxy.ServiceProxy to forward `app.start()` etc. to a separate class implementing the service:

```
class App(ServiceProxy):

   @cached_property
   def _service(self):
       return AppService(app=self)

class AppService(Service):

    def __init__(self, app, **kwargs):
        self.app = app
        super().__init__(**kwargs)

    def on_start(self):
           await self.add_runtime_dependency(self.consumer)
           await self.add_runtime_dependency(self.producer)
```

If you don't create apps at module scope there is no reason to use ServiceProxy.

The worker we have separate, so the app does not inherit from worker, instead the worker starts the app:

```
if __name__ == '__main__':
    Worker(Application(), loglevel='INFO', logfile=None).execute_from_commandline()
```

Using `logfile=None` means it will log to stderr.
Passing the logfile argument is all you need to setup the logging really. It will configure the root logger,
and any other logger will propagate to the root logger.

I don't know of any bech32 pip installable library to use. Maybe we can find one. Sipa's reference code does the job well.

I have added commits which among others improve the fee estimation, see #44 . The fix adds to the Unspents-objects additional fields to denote the type (p2pkh/p2sh/np2wkh, etc.). 

Further, regarding #30 I have implemented the coin selection method "Branch and Bound" to try to find a perfect match when combine=False (with no return address) and otherwise falls back to a single random draw with return address with a threshold for a minimum value for the return address if desired. It uses random exploration, but it may be better to use largest first exploration, see discussion here:
https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/10637
For Bit I don't think it makes a big difference (using largest first exploration would actually be easier). I have not tested the performance extensively using uge unspent-sets, which should still be done.

Then I have added/changed some of the tests to make them more specific.

Lastly there is still one small thing missing, which is a correct tx-id calculation. With segwit the current calculate_txid() function will calculate the hash for segwit transactions, but not what is now considered the txid. As far as I can see the function is only called to display the  id when sending a transaction. It would probably make sense to refactor the code for calculating the transaction id to the TxObj instead. 

If you have any questions or suggestions for improvement let me know after you have had the time to take a look at the pull request.

The note is related to the fact that the codebase is mostly C and thus can have bugs that crash your whole Python interpreter.
Also I used it only during assets preparation phase of web applications (so at "compile time") so never had a DukPy app running longterm to check eventual leaks, nor I ever had a chance to test it on a real multithreaded environment to see how if race conditions or similar problems arise.

Until users or myself had a chance to test it in those three conditions I wouldn't consider DukPy safe for production use.

Note that `urlparse.urljoin` is not workable for this stuff because it only joins if the second argument doesn't begin with `/` and the first argument ends with `/`, which is just not how the concept of "prefix" works.

The more framerate the less time value should be because there will be more text per second.
Frame = Time * Framerate. Frame is the same, Framerate changes, we need to calculate new Time:
T_new * F_new = T_old * F_old
T_new = T_old * (F_old/F_new)
Since Ratio = F_new/F_old then
T_new = T_old * (1/Ratio)
T_new = T_old/Ratio

I am not sure to see where this could lead to a slow down. Do you have a particular case in mind ?

> Strictly speaking, dBm is a dimensionless unit and somehow breaks the logic that conversion can only be done between quantities with the same dimensionality. 

dBm is in watts, not dimensionless. dBV and dBu are in volts, dBSPL is in pascal, etc.

Bare decibel (and bel), neper, octave, decade, semitone, etc are dimensionless units used to express relative differences on logarithmic scales, but the units with suffixes are absolute units with dimensions.

- 0 dBm = 1 mW
- 0 dBW = 1 W
- 0 dBV = 1 V
- 6 dBV ≈ 2 V
- 3 dBW ≈ 2 W
- 0 dBSPL = 20 μPa

[The weirdness of V/V ratios being different from W/W ratios](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decibel#Definition) would probably require that the units not cancel out, though?  I don't know how this would be supported.

```python
PLUGINS = [
    # Built-ins

]
```

Changed paths_to_polygons in trimesh.path.polygons. Removed call to
repair_invalid in trimesh.path.path2D.polygons_full.
Notes:
1) Tolerances used are very specific to my use case - slicing stl design
files and making appropriate laser paths for 3D printing. Physical units
are mm i.e. a distance tolerance of 0.1 would be a tolerance of 100 microns.
2) If a polygon is valid, it's taken as is. If not, two options: a) if
simplification doesn't work at least-stringent tolerance, use buffering;
else, b) find most-stringent simplification tolerance (>0.1 micron) and
simplify. Keep polygons with area > 0.01 mm^2, even if invalid - will be
handled downstream.
3) Method seems to be quite robust - haven't had any floating-point,
topology or invalidity issues for 100+ designs over the last couple months.
4) Maybe this issue is better handled upstream by setting different
tolerances during path construction - I haven't looked into that. Also not
sure how these tolerances will affect other use cases.
5) Most of the issues I've had, particularly with reference to
intersections between LineString & Polygon, arise from 'valid' polygons
which have (very thin) folds in their external and/or internal contours.
I've taken to doing a default simplification (or buffer) of all
polygons_closed to avoid this. Again, not sure how this translates to other
use cases.

Best,
Ashwin Nagaraj

Generally, it is not a good practice to read `requirements.txt` from `setup.py` and `install_requires` should be listed in `setup.py` separately from `requirements.txt`. There is a really good explanation for this [on StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/a/33685899)

There are three possible solutions:
1. List requirements in `setup.py` separately from `requirements.txt` in a proper way.
2. Just change current code a bit to adapt to pip changes: they have moved `req` to `_internal`, so something like `from pip._internal import req` should work. But that is a hack, not a good solution :)
3. Just read requirements.txt as file and use its lines for `install_requires`. Some repos followed this way: [example](https://github.com/NordicSemiconductor/pc-nrfutil/pull/94/files).

I am unsure what the extended status code 8445 means; as with almost everything EtherNet/IP CIP, there is little to no documentation available (correction: there is vast quantities of documentation available from the ODVA: but it is almost entirely useless).

I would recommend using the cpppo.server.enip.get_attribute "proxy" API.  The cpppo.server.enip.client "connector" API is very low-level; it is designed for issuing and harvesting a stream of requests to/from a device, in a pipelined fashion.  Usually, this is a bit lower level than you'd want to be operating...

See server/enip/poll_example_many_with_write.py for an example of how to repetitively poll values from a device, and occasionally write new values without interrupting the polling.  Of course, you can just perform the writes without the polling...

second from the end out[-2] it is version, regardless of package name

See issue #62 .  I believe one of the problems is that "parameter arrays" require the input data to be converted to a fixed-width encoding, i.e. NOT utf-8 or utf-16, because the data has to be temporarily stored in a block of memory, in grid-like fashion.  This means converting the input data to something like UCS-16 (with a small but significant possibility of data loss/corruption), and having to figure out how big the grid should be.  Not an easy problem to solve, but it would certainly help me in the work I do.

Like most of the other objects, the ScheduleObject is just a framework for knowing what properties it has and their datatype.  An implementation would be something like the `LocalDeviceObject` that builds on the framework, and that doesn't exist.  If you would like to write one, it would be something like this:

```
    class LocalScheduleObject(ScheduleObject, Task):
        ...
        def __init__(self, **kwargs):
            ...
            self.install_task(0)

        def process_task(self):
            # code goes here
            self.presentValue = current_value
            self.install_task(next_transition)
```

The idea is that the schedule "runs" whenever the value is supposed to change based on the current time, then schedules itself to run again the next time it is supposed to change.  This is non-trivial because of all the special values that `Date` objects can have, in your case you are only using the `weeklySchedule` so it could be easier.

If the `weeklySchedule` is expected to be writable, then you'll need a hook to "run" it by calling `deferred(obj.process_task)` or `obj.install_task(0)` when the property value changes.  That is much easier to write in the upcoming version (0.15.0) than it is now.

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
A Translation module.

You can translate text using this module.
"""
from time import sleep
from random import random
import logging
import requests

from googletrans import urls, utils
from googletrans.compat import PY3
from googletrans.gtoken import TokenAcquirer
from googletrans.constants import DEFAULT_USER_AGENT, LANGUAGES, SPECIAL_CASES
from googletrans.models import Translated, Detected

import requests_cache

# cache file set to googletrans_cache.sqlite if not already set in previous import requests_cache  # noqa
requests_cache.install_cache('googletrans_cache')

LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__name__)
LOGGER.addHandler(logging.NullHandler())

EXCLUDES = ('en', 'ca', 'fr')


class Translator(object):
    """Google Translate ajax API implementation class

    You have to create an instance of Translator to use this API

    :param service_urls: google translate url list. URLs will be used randomly.
                         For example ``['translate.google.com', 'translate.google.co.kr']``
    :type service_urls: a sequence of strings

    :param user_agent: the User-Agent header to send when making requests.
    :type user_agent: :class:`str`
    """

    def __init__(self, service_urls=None, user_agent=DEFAULT_USER_AGENT):
        self.session = requests.Session()
        self.session.headers.update({
            'User-Agent': user_agent,
        })
        self.token_acquirer = TokenAcquirer(session=self.session)
        # self.service_urls = service_urls or ['translate.google.com']
        self.service_urls = service_urls or ['translate.google.cn']

        self.from_cache = False

        # Use HTTP2 Adapter if hyper is installed
        try:  # pragma: nocover
            from hyper.contrib import HTTP20Adapter
            self.session.mount(urls.BASE, HTTP20Adapter())
        except ImportError:  # pragma: nocover
            pass

    def _pick_service_url(self):
        if len(self.service_urls) == 1:
            return self.service_urls[0]
        return random.choice(self.service_urls)

    def _translate(self, text, dest='en', src='auto'):
        if src != 'auto':
            if src not in LANGUAGES.keys() and src in SPECIAL_CASES.keys():
                src = SPECIAL_CASES[src]
            elif src not in LANGUAGES.keys():
                raise ValueError('invalid source language')

        if dest not in LANGUAGES.keys():
            if dest in SPECIAL_CASES.keys():
                dest = SPECIAL_CASES[dest]
            else:
                raise ValueError('invalid destination language')

        if not PY3 and isinstance(text, str):  # pragma: nocover
            text = text.decode('utf-8')

        token = self.token_acquirer.do(text)
        params = utils.build_params(query=text, src=src, dest=dest,
                                    token=token)
        url = urls.TRANSLATE.format(host=self._pick_service_url())
        r = self.session.get(url, params=params)

        self.from_cache = r.from_cache
        LOGGER.debug(" from_cache: %s", self.from_cache)

        data = utils.format_json(r.text)
        return data

    def translate(self, text, dest='en', src='auto', delay=None):
        """Translate text from source language to destination language

        :param text: The source text(s) to be translated. Batch translation is supported via sequence input.
        :type text: UTF-8 :class:`str`; :class:`unicode`; string sequence (list, tuple, iterator, generator)

        :param dest: The language to translate the source text into.
                     The value should be one of the language codes listed in :const:`googletrans.LANGUAGES`.
        :param dest: :class:`str`; :class:`unicode`

        :param src: The language of the source text.
                    The value should be one of the language codes listed in :const:`googletrans.LANGUAGES`.
                    If a language is not specified,
                    the system will attempt to identify the source language automatically.
        :param src: :class:`str`; :class:`unicode`

        :rtype: Translated
        :rtype: :class:`list` (when a list is passed)

        Basic usage:
            >>> from googletrans import Translator
            >>> translator = Translator()
            >>> translator.translate('안녕하세요.')
            <Translated src=ko dest=en text=Good evening. pronunciation=Good evening.>
            >>> translator.translate('안녕하세요.', dest='ja')
            <Translated src=ko dest=ja text=こんにちは。 pronunciation=Kon'nichiwa.>
            >>> translator.translate('veritas lux mea', src='la')
            <Translated src=la dest=en text=The truth is my light pronunciation=The truth is my light>

        Advanced usage:
            >>> translations = translator.translate(['The quick brown fox', 'jumps over', 'the lazy dog'], dest='ko')
            >>> for translation in translations:
            ...    print(translation.origin, ' -> ', translation.text)
            The quick brown fox  ->  빠른 갈색 여우
            jumps over  ->  이상 점프
            the lazy dog  ->  게으른 개
        """
        if delay:
            if isinstance(delay, (int, float)):
                delay = min(0.5, delay)
            else:
                delay = 0.5

        if isinstance(text, list):
            result = []
            for item in text:
                translated = self.translate(item, dest=dest, src=src)
                result.append(translated)
                if not self.from_cache and delay:  # if delay is set, sleep random secs (mean=delay) if response is not from cache  # noqa
                    sleep_time = delay - 0.5 + random()
                    LOGGER.debug(" sleep %.2f sec", sleep_time)
                    sleep(sleep_time)
            return result

        origin = text
        data = self._translate(text, dest, src)

        # this code will be updated when the format is changed.
        translated = ''.join([d[0] if d[0] else '' for d in data[0]])

        # actual source language that will be recognized by Google Translator when the
        # src passed is equal to auto.
        try:
            src = data[-1][0][0]
        except Exception:  # pragma: nocover
            pass

        pron = origin
        try:
            pron = data[0][1][-1]
        except Exception:  # pragma: nocover
            pass
        if not PY3 and isinstance(pron, unicode) and isinstance(origin, str):  # pragma: nocover
            origin = origin.decode('utf-8')
        if dest in EXCLUDES and pron == origin:
            pron = translated

        # for python 2.x compatbillity
        if not PY3:  # pragma: nocover
            if isinstance(src, str):
                src = src.decode('utf-8')
            if isinstance(dest, str):
                dest = dest.decode('utf-8')
            if isinstance(translated, str):
                translated = translated.decode('utf-8')

        # put final values into a new Translated object
        result = Translated(src=src, dest=dest, origin=origin,
                            text=translated, pronunciation=pron)

        return result

    def detect(self, text):
        """Detect language of the input text

        :param text: The source text(s) whose language you want to identify.
                     Batch detection is supported via sequence input.
        :type text: UTF-8 :class:`str`; :class:`unicode`; string sequence (list, tuple, iterator, generator)

        :rtype: Detected
        :rtype: :class:`list` (when a list is passed)

        Basic usage:
            >>> from googletrans import Translator
            >>> translator = Translator()
            >>> translator.detect('이 문장은 한글로 쓰여졌습니다.')
            <Detected lang=ko confidence=0.27041003>
            >>> translator.detect('この文章は日本語で書かれました。')
            <Detected lang=ja confidence=0.64889508>
            >>> translator.detect('This sentence is written in English.')
            <Detected lang=en confidence=0.22348526>
            >>> translator.detect('Tiu frazo estas skribita en Esperanto.')
            <Detected lang=eo confidence=0.10538048>

        Advanced usage:
            >>> langs = translator.detect(['한국어', '日本語', 'English', 'le français'])
            >>> for lang in langs:
            ...    print(lang.lang, lang.confidence)
            ko 1
            ja 0.92929292
            en 0.96954316
            fr 0.043500196
        """
        if isinstance(text, list):
            result = []
            for item in text:
                lang = self.detect(item)
                result.append(lang)
            return result

        data = self._translate(text, dest='en', src='auto')

        # actual source language that will be recognized by Google Translator when the
        # src passed is equal to auto.
        src = ''
        confidence = 0.0
        try:
            src = ''.join(data[8][0])
            confidence = data[8][-2][0]
        except Exception:  # pragma: nocover
            pass
        result = Detected(lang=src, confidence=confidence)

        return result

On Mon, Mar 16, 2015 at 5:21 PM, John McNamara <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> Hi,
>
> It took a while to get around to working on support for combined charts in
> XlsxWriter so I'm happy to report some good progress in time for St.
> Patrick's day.
>
> There is a beta branch (combined_chart
> <https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/tree/combined_chart>) with
> support for combined charts. You can now create 2 charts and add one to the
> other to combine them like this:
>
> import xlsxwriter
>
> workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('combined_chart1.xlsx')
>
> worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()
>
> chart1 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'column'})
> chart2 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'line'})
> # Some data to write to the chart.
> data = [
>     [2, 7, 3, 6, 2],
>     [20, 25, 10, 10, 20],
> ]
>
> worksheet.write_column('A1', data[0])
> worksheet.write_column('B1', data[1])
> # The primary chart.
> chart1.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$A$1:$A$5'})
> # The secondary chart.
> chart2.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$B$1:$B$5'})
> # Add the secondary chart to the primary chart.
> chart1.combine(chart2)
> # Insert it in the worksheet.
> worksheet.insert_chart('E9', chart1)
>
> workbook.close()
>
> [image: screenshot]
> <https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/94267/6676639/5c13248e-cc21-11e4-8299-01d0c2439cb6.png>
>
> Or place the secondary chart on a secondary axis, using the standard
> syntax:
>
> import xlsxwriter
>
> workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('combined_chart2.xlsx')
>
> worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()
>
> chart1 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'column'})
> chart2 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'line'})
> # Some data to write to the chart.
> data = [
>     [2, 7, 3, 6, 2],
>     [20, 25, 10, 10, 20],
> ]
>
> worksheet.write_column('A1', data[0])
> worksheet.write_column('B1', data[1])
> # The primary chart.
> chart1.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$A$1:$A$5'})
> # The secondary chart.
> chart2.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$B$1:$B$5', 'y2_axis': 1})
> # Add the secondary chart to the primary chart.
> chart1.combine(chart2)
> # Insert it in the worksheet.
> worksheet.insert_chart('E9', chart1)
>
> workbook.close()
>
> [image: screenshot]
> <https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/94267/6676666/97753d96-cc21-11e4-91e7-6bdbdb74e80b.png>
>
> Both charts can be configured separately before or after they are combined.
>
> The new chart.combine() method is the only addition. Everything else is
> the standard chart interface.
>
> *Terms and Conditions Apply*
>
> This is beta code. It needs a lot more test cases and it is quite possible
> to break things. Also, scatter charts aren't support yet, but will be
> shortly.
>
> If you'd like to test it out then please do and let me know if you
> encounter any issues.
>
> John

The example usages for the playback callback funcs have been updated to hopefully be a little more clear. There is also now an example skill showing one way to play through a playlist.

One thing to note is that the PlayBackNearlyFinished Request is usually sent almost immediately after you send a PlayDirective. The name of the request is a little deceptive, but it just means Alexa will allow a new stream to be enqueued.

These callbacks can accept any top-level parameters of the request body, even if not mapped. Although offset is mapped by default to offsetInMilliSeconds for convenience.
The request_body never includes a url however, so that would need be accessed through the current_stream local's 'url' attribute.

You will see in the playlist example a QueueManager class. Something like this is required to keep track of queue order and which url is currently being streamed. But from your examples, it seems like you have come up with something similar.

Hi,

Sorry I didn't attend to this issue for a while. I was held up in other projects. In any case, I didn't give it much importance since initial research into the TLS specs showed that this is not actually an error.

After no longer being held up by other projects and some more research, I'm sure it actually is an error. I'm in the process of implementing this now. I've settled for this behaviour:

* For creating child CAs, the maximum expiry is silently set to that of its parent. The reason is that any default of X days is automatically after that of the parent a day after issuing the parrent.
* For child certificates, I throw an error if the default or whatever the user specifies is after the CAs expiry.

This means that with a default of a year and a CA expiring in 10 years, you'll be able to silently issue certs for nine years, in the tenth year you'll get errors with the default expiry. This seems reasonable because an expiring CA is something you really should take care of. In the meant time, you can issue shorter-lived certs if you explicitly want.

@jwcraftsman Hi and thanks for the interest in parglare.

I agree with @alberth that keeping state seems complicated. I don't know the details to reason about whether you are on the right path. Maybe that's the only way to do it. I haven't put much thought in this issue yet.

What I would like is to make those indent/column constraints a part of the grammar, thus making it more explicit and visible.

Here are some of the ideas I was thinking about:
- For column based languages we could have a special recognizer `column(x)` where `x` is the column. This recognizer would succeed without consuming any input only if called with the position at the given column.
Thus we could write something like:

```
Statement :  column(7) 'SomeKeyword' ....;
```
`Statement` could be reduced only if `SomeKeyword` is found on column 7, i.e. if `column(7)` recognizer succeeds.

- For indentation languages (Python, YAML etc.) similar approach could be taken. For example, lets say we have a special recognizer `indent(x)` which recognizes indent level and returns token with the indent level stored inside.

Lets look at the 'if' statement:
```python
    if condition:
        if_body
    else:
        else_body
```
We could write the grammar rule:
```
IfStatement: indent(a) 'if' condition 
                   indent(a+1) if_body 
                   indent(a) 'else:' 
                   indent(a+1) else_body;
```
Each `indent` recognizer produces `indent` token with the indent level remembered and connected to the expression inside the parentheses. When the time for reduction comes on the LR stack we have:

```
[ indent(a), 'if', condition, indent(a+1), if_body, indent(a), 'else:', indent(a+1), else_body ]
```
In order for the parser to reduce this to `if` statement all `indent` token expressions must be solved so that a single value for `a` is found and that each expression in parentheses equals to the stored indentation level.

As I said, I haven't give it much thought yet so these ideas are probably full of holes. I'm not sure that with this approach I would still be in the realm of the deterministic LR parsing as the "state" of the parser would now depend on the stored indentation levels in `indent` tokens. The table calculation would probably be influenced greatly also.

Anyway, I don't want to discourage you. The insights you make along the way could be valuable to us. 

As a tip, there is a `Context` object that is passed around. If you give an instance to the parser it will use the same instance during the course of parsing. It is passed to actions, it is not passed to recognizers yet but you could easily hack that to try your approach. Try first to implement it with LR to avoid dealing with GLR complexity.

Happy hacking! :)

There are a few options here, depending on your use case it might be easiest to instantiate a pool of consul agents and use it per process or use one agent per process. The latter would require you to concede the most performance hit and is probably wasteful. If suitable, you can also look at using the `consul.aio.Consul` (python 3.4.2+), `consul.tornado.Consul` or `consul.twisted.Consul` implementations of the agent.

Check out [this single-frame example](https://github.com/kkroening/ffmpeg-python/tree/master/examples#read-single-video-frame-as-jpeg-through-pipe) and [numpy array example](https://github.com/kkroening/ffmpeg-python/tree/master/examples#convert-video-to-numpy-array).

I recommend working with the video in something like numpy since it's much easier to work with, unless you have some other use case that precludes this.

Here's one of the easiest ways to read a single frame:
```python
out, _ = (
    ffmpeg
    .input('in.mp4')
    .filter('select', 'gte(n, {})'.format(frame_num))
    .output('pipe:', format='rawvideo', pix_fmt='rgb24', vframes=1)
    .run(capture_stdout=True)
)
video = (
    np
    .frombuffer(out, np.uint8)
    .reshape([-1, height, width, 3])
)
```
One quick 'n dirty way to do this would be to wrap the above and exec ffmpeg for each frame. Depending on the number of frames you're trying to extract, this is somewhere on the spectrum between "entirely practical" and "totally insane and inefficient."

A slightly improved version would use input seeking (ffmpeg `-ss` option) so that ffmpeg goes straight to the frame you want instead of twiddling its thumbs skipping over the initial frames you don't want. Depending on your use case, this might be the best bet, with the caveat that `-ss` only takes timecodes and not frame numbers (if I understand correctly; could be wrong though), so you have to do some careful math and deal with rounding errors to get the exact frame you want.

If you don't use `-ss` I don't know that there's any way to have ffmpeg actually skip over the frames you don't want. It basically has to go through every single frame of the video and then filter out the frames you don't want, using something like the `select` operator. If you're pulling out a large percentage of the total number of frames, say 20%, then this is perfectly reasonable. But if you're pulling a handful of frames from a 2 hour video, then it's probably best to just run ffmpeg either multiple times with a different `-ss` or with multiple inputs (each with different `-ss` params).

The best bet is to refer to the examples and look closely at the ffmpeg documentation. There isn't a one-size-fits-all for this so you'll have to see what works best for your particular use case.

With very simple loops such as this one the overhead can indeed be quite significant. As soon as you add a `progressbar.Timer()` the progress bar will have to fetch the system time for every update. Since you are executing this `2000000` times it will definitely have an impact.

I would recommend updating the progress bar less often, you won't notice the difference and the impact will be far smaller. It seems like this is something I should actually add as a feature, but for the time being.

If you modify your code to look something like this it will be far better:
```
widgets = [progressbar.Percentage(), progressbar.Bar(), progressbar.Timer()]
bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(widgets=widgets, max_value=range_count).start()
timer_start = time.time()
for i in range(range_count):
    values = [x for x in range(100)]
    del values[95]
    # Only update once for every 10000 values
    if i % 10000 == 0:
        bar.update(i)
bar.finish()
timer_stop = time.time()
print('\ndel - done in %i s' %(timer_stop - timer_start))
```

You are right regarding the radio buttons - the native applications move the selection with up/left/down/right arrows.

I checked MS Outlook configuration dialog and it behaves like the following:

  1. Keys up or left move focus to the previous item. Keys down or right move focus to the next item.
  2. When there is a special meaning for the key (i.e. the key event is eaten by the widget), the focus movement is disabled:
       a) Single-line text boxes - cannot be leaved with left/right, but could be with up/down.
       b) Multi-line text boxes - cannot be leaved at all.
       c) Radio buttons - cannot be leaved at all, cursor movement changes the selection immediately and does not allow you to leave the group.
       d) Combo boxes - cannot be leaved with up/down, because it operates the popup list. If combo box contain editable text, it cannot be leaved with left/right (because it changes cursor position inside the combo box).

Linux Qt4 (I checked examples in the qt4-demo application) shows very similar behaviour with one exception - single-line text box cannot be leaved with up/down keys (which do nothing).

Midnight Commander under Linux console is little bit different story here, it does not change the radio button selection at all, the movement is not limited, and for combo boxes there is a special key to open the popup list, which is different to cursor keys. Maybe I would just skip this as an irrelevant example.

So from the implementation point of view (not the user point of view) the behaviour of Windows and Qt4 looks logical to me - whenever the widget has a special meaning for the key (widget is also radio button group in this point of view!), let it eat the event. If the event is not consumed, do the focus change.

So I would do the same:

  1. Do not allow leaving the radio group, because you should change the selection inside the group with up/left and right/down keys, not jump out of the group.
  2. Between layers I would simply go to the first element (top left) in the forward-TAB direction, and to the last element (bottom right) in the reversed-TAB direction. This should be the easiest.

If somebody wants to jump to the closest-positioned column on the button down press between layers, he would need to tune the implementation in some derived class. This behaviour might be wanted only sometimes - for example in the movement between multiple different tables, but could not be wanted for movement to the last row of buttons with Save/Reset/Cancel actions - it always depends on the author's intention. From the API point of view it should be sufficient to have a method that allows the modification (with the possibility to get the currently focused element to know the positions).

[Try setting the `convert_image` argument when calling `convert_to_html`](https://github.com/mwilliamson/python-mammoth#custom-image-handlers).

For instance, the CLI passes the below as `convert_image`:

```python
convert_image = mammoth.images.inline(ImageWriter(args.output_dir))

class ImageWriter(object):
    def __init__(self, output_dir):
        self._output_dir = output_dir
        self._image_number = 1
        
    def __call__(self, element):
        extension = element.content_type.partition("/")[2]
        image_filename = "{0}.{1}".format(self._image_number, extension)
        with open(os.path.join(self._output_dir, image_filename), "wb") as image_dest:
            with element.open() as image_source:
                shutil.copyfileobj(image_source, image_dest)
        
        self._image_number += 1
        
        return {"src": image_filename}
```

You can use SQL Server LocalDB with django-pyodbc-azure. I always use LocalDB for developing this backend because it's fast and handy. Here's an example of setting up:

1) Install LocalDB (and an ODBC driver if necessary)

2) Run the following commands as a Administrator
```
> sqllocaldb create SQLLOCAL
> sqllocaldb share SQLLOCAL SQLLOCALSHARED
> sqllocaldb start SQLLOCAL
> sqllocaldb info SQLLOCAL
> sqlcmd -S (named pipe name described in the info command above)
1> create login mydjangouser with password='password'
2> go
1> sp_addsrvrolemember mydjangouser, sysadmin
2> go
1> create user mydjangouser
2> go
1> create database mydb
2> go
1> exit
```

3) Update DATABASES settings for your Django app
```py
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'sql_server.pyodbc',
        'NAME': 'mydb',
        'USER': 'mydjangouser',
        'PASSWORD': 'password',
        'HOST': '(localdb)\\.\\SQLLOCALSHARED',
        'PORT': '',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'driver': 'ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server',
        },
    },
}
```

Note that you can't connect to LocalDB with Microsoft ODBC Driver 13 (Preview) for SQL Server on Windows as of writing. I'm using Microsoft ODBC Driver 11 to connect to SQL Server 2016 LocalDB now as a workaround. I hope the issue would be resolved by Microsoft in the future.

``(new) introduce environment variable isolation: tox now only passes the PATH and PIP_INDEX_URL variable from the tox invocation environment to the test environment and on Windows also SYSTEMROOT, PATHEXT, TEMP and TMP whereas on unix additionally TMPDIR is passed. If you need to pass through further environment variables you can use the new passenv setting, a space-separated list of environment variable names. Each name can make use of fnmatch-style glob patterns. All environment variables which exist in the tox-invocation environment will be copied to the test environment.``

Definitely worth doing something to let the region (and any other BROKER_TRANSPORT_OPTIONS) get set on Celery-- else airflow can only deployed with SQS in us-east. Deploying with SQS is particularly useful because SQS queue size can be tied in to increasing/decreasing the size of an autoscaling group of airflow workers.

I agree raising an exception would be the proper thing to do. Regarding the issue with tasks that return large xcoms:

> you must change your task behaviour for your DAG to terminate

I think this is the answer. Anything else would be trying to accomodate use cases that are beyond the scope of airflow. To quote [the airflow docs](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/docs/index.rst#beyond-the-horizon):

> Airflow is not a data streaming solution. Tasks do not move data from one to the other (though tasks can exchange metadata!).

If you have tasks that are returning values larger than some reasonable xcom size limit, you probably should rethink how your DAGs are working.

The problem here is that TensorFlow doesn't know how to properly freeze a graph that uses batch norm. I'm focusing on getting the training side of ds2-v2 running perfectly before working on exporting and etc, so I won't be able to debug TensorFlow very soon.

i have solved it.
**_NOTE on gcc 5 or later: the binary pip packages available on the TensorFlow website are built with gcc 4, which uses the older
ABI. To make your build compatible with the older ABI, you need to add --cxxopt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0" to your
bazel build command. ABI compatibility allows custom ops built against the TensorFlow pip package to continue to work against your built package._**

so, you should check version of gcc. if it  is 5 or later, you should add _--cxxopt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX!!_ABI=0"_

when you compile tensorflow by yourself, use bazel build _-cxxopt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0" --config=opt //tensorflow/tools/pip_package:build_pip_package_
then you use _bazel build -cxxopt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0" -c opt --copt=-march=native --copt=-mtune=native --copt=-O3 //tensorflow:libtensorflow_cc.so //native_client:*_
at last, you use _bazel build -cxxopt="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0"  //native_client:ctc_decoder_with_kenlm_
succeed!

> So, if group is not a standard SAML attribute

Sort of. In my case this field is actually called `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/claims/Group` and, for example, email field is called `http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/emailaddress`. To make it more human-friendly, pysaml2 uses so-called attribute_maps which can (and should be) configured. That's why if you have something like groups in your claims, you can always make it to be called `'groups'`. That's why `SAML2_GROUP_ATTR` parameter from your example is redundant. In the rest, I agree with you and will rewrite it to `ALLOWED_SAML2_GROUPS`.

So, what do you think about `role` vs. `groups` in `g` and JWT token? The problem is that current alerta roles is not flexible enough but adding competing groups would be inconsistent. I'm thinking about refactoring roles somehow to be more flexible but do not have solution yet.

Speaking as a customer I would really appreciate this being built-in, for two reasons.

One is that managing and keeping track of lots of secrets in plain-text files on disk is a security nightmare.  By contrast — on macOS, at least — locking down the keychain is as easy as the "show keychain status in menu bar" option and glancing up there to see what state things are in.

Another is to prevent mistakes.  I have tests for my automation tooling, and I want to force them to talk to mocks, and not make real AWS connections, even if I'm running them on my own account.  I can set up Keychain to prompt me for each access to credentials for a Python process, and if my tests start popping that dialog I know I've made a mistake, and I can fix it before any damage is done.

Waitress specifically works like this: "When a channel determines the client has sent at least one full valid HTTP request, it schedules a “task” with a “thread dispatcher”. The thread dispatcher maintains a fixed pool of worker threads available to do client work (by default, 4 threads). If a worker thread is available when a task is scheduled, the worker thread runs the task. The task has access to the channel, and can write back to the channel’s output buffer. When all worker threads are in use, scheduled tasks will wait in a queue for a worker thread to become available." Thus it would be just a matter of being allowed to configure a queueing policy where the supplied default works like now.

@Byron I am the primary maintainer of SaltStack's gitfs, which makes use of GitPython.

What kind of performance implications? Also, could you clarify what you mean by "custom gitdb implementation"?

The way we use GitPython is as follows:

1. Try to ``git.Repo('/path/to/repo')`` the desired path. If no repo is initialized at that path, instantiate the Repo object using ``git.Repo.init()``.
2. If the repo was just init'ed in the previous step, we add a remote using ``git.Repo.create_remote()``, pointing it at the desired repo.

We then proceed to fetch occasionally to keep the repo up-to-date, and when a file is requested, it is retrieved by finding the Tree object corresponding to the newest commit in the desired branch (or for tags, the commit corresponding to the tag). The blob for the desired file is retrieved from the tree, and then the file's data is retrieved from that blob object.

By the way: a quick workaround should be to leave GitPython out of the equation and just run `repo.git.fetch()`. That way, you will not have any branch information, but the fetch will definitely work.
The `Repo.clone_from()` operation can also be approximated by using the `Git` type directly.

## Controller flexibility

I agree that this is something that magicbot is not good at. I've contemplated ways to address this problem, but haven't really hit on anything that I really liked.

It would be trivial to implement a similar framework (or co-op the existing framework perhaps) that could cooperate with magicbot components, notionally something like:

```python
def createObjects(self):
  self.button = Button(joystick=1, button=3)
  self.button.whenPressed(self.fireComponent.shoot)
```

The key would be to ensure that the button/etc components run first, so that they can trigger actions in the other components.

## Event flexibility

This seems to be the same as above, or are there other event things in Command-based?

## Concurrency, Conflict management

I'll have to think about a good response for these. Magicbot doesn't explicitly address these, but I think if one adheres to the magicbot philosophy then these are actually less of an issue. More documentation would help.

## Reordering steps

These are valid criticisms.

## Dashboard feedback

I extensively use NetworkTables for feedback, and have found SmartDashboard extraordinarily clunky for dealing with large numbers of variables, so I end up using TableViewer exclusively instead. 

Magicbot provides extensive networktables integration, see [this section of the docs](http://robotpy-wpilib-utilities.readthedocs.io/en/latest/magicbot.html#dashboard-coprocessor-communications). Though it's not documented very well, each state machine provides network tables variables that can be used to tune the state machine.

## Command based mitigations

I welcome pull requests that address these issues.

Another case is that we allow text pairs in the `rawValues` argument to `normalizeRawHeadersMutable`, which by extension also means to the `__init__` methods for `FrozenHTTPHeaders` and `MutableHTTPHeaders`.  It's fairly permissive:

```python
headers = FrozenHTTPHeaders(
    (u"Content-Type", u"text/html; charset=utf-8"),
    (b"ETag", b"yo"),
    (u"X-Fubar", binary_crap_omg),  # for the haters
)
```

The final value for `headers.rawHeaders` will be tuples of bytes, so having the `__init__` argument not accept that is weird.  But since I think the common case for the caller is to use text, forcing the caller to encode (to Latin 1) seems unfortunate.  Adding factory methods (`fromTextPairs`, `fromBytesPairs`) would work.

The problem is a type mismatch for  the function `healpix_rangesearch_radec_simple()`

* It is defined as `int64_t` in [`healpix_utils.c` ](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/528292bc2bff01563d6c696880020345cde88d40/cextern/astrometry.net/healpix-utils.c#L142-L143),
* but declared as `int` in 
  - [`healpix-utils.h`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/528292bc2bff01563d6c696880020345cde88d40/cextern/astrometry.net/healpix-utils.h#L18) and
  - [`core_cython.pxd`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/528292bc2bff01563d6c696880020345cde88d40/astropy_healpix/core_cython.pxd#L41).
* When it is used in [`core_cython.pyx`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/528292bc2bff01563d6c696880020345cde88d40/astropy_healpix/core_cython.pyx#L485), the function is assumed to return an `int` (32 bit), and it seems to cut the lower bits on mips in this case.

When I change the return type in the definition ([`healpix_utils.c`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/528292bc2bff01563d6c696880020345cde88d40/cextern/astrometry.net/healpix-utils.c#L142-L143)) to `int`, this problem would be solved. However, the change to `int64_t` was probably done in fd48d256364a33d37950c41e95736db6810e9f33 on purpose.

IMO the declarations should be fixed here, and somehow a declaration should be included in `core_cython.pyx` -- just changing them to `int64_t` did _not_ solve the problem. And I am not really knowledgable in Cython to do that properly.

So I've done a mistake - I've forgot to cast and pad the image appropriately, before calculating the sums. I've corrected this now (I added the first part of the script, and got somewhat different results, which are more in line with my previous assumptions about integral images. These are the comparisons of 'sum of value' and 'sum of squared value' images calculated using integral image and convolution. It is infact the sum of squares that has a large error at the bottom, where the integral image is losing precision in a 32 bit floating point value. Convolution does not have the same
problem, as it doesn't need to sum all the values in those columns, but sums only the values inside the windows (either directly or using DFT).

from pyramid.events import NewRequest

```

It seems as if most of pyfolio is already independent of benchmarks. Most functionality in `timeseries.py` and `plotting.py` already check if `benchmark_rets=None`. So this is not as painful a PR as we initially expected, but there's still a lot to do.

So the thing we appear to be testing here is that the beta of a returns stream against a constant benchmark is 0. If I put a breakpoint in empyrical, the values being passed for the day that's failing this test are:

```
(Pdb++) returns
2000-01-05    0.1
2000-01-06    0.0
2000-01-07    0.0
Freq: D, Name: 0, dtype: float64
(Pdb++) factor_returns
2000-01-05    0.0
2000-01-06    0.0
2000-01-07    0.0
Freq: D, Name: 1, dtype: float64
```

The "mathematically correct" result for this beta calculation is 0, but due to floating point rounding it's unlikely that you'll get exactly 0 from any implementation (you'll only get zero if the sum of a sequence of floats that should add up to zero actually adds up to **exactly** zero, which is unlikely) The test is currently asserting that we emit a beta of exactly `8.024708101613483e-32`, which appears to have been copied from the result that `scipy.stats.linregress` produces. The new vectorized beta fuinction in empyrical uses a different algorithm, and thus produces a different (also very small) value.

I think the right fix for this test is to change the assertion to use `np.assert_almost_equal` or a similar "tolerant equal" check to assert that the beta we get here is zero.

Separately, I see two issues with the current rolling_beta implementation that are probably worth investigating further:

1. The handling of `rolling_window` appears to be off by 1.  The test is calling `rolling_beta` with a window of 2, but stepping through the code we appear to be operating on windows of length 3.
2. `rolling_beta` is implemented by repeatedly calling `empyrical.beta` on hand-computed slices from its input arrays, but `empyrical` already has a `roll_beta` function, which is about 200x faster:

```
In [13]: %timeit pf.rolling_beta(returns, factor, 252)
1 loops, best of 3: 521 ms per loop

In [14]: %timeit ep.stats.roll_beta(returns, factor, 252)
100 loops, best of 3: 2.19 ms per loop
```

I'd be happy to have my passport icon included as part of Font Awesome (and would more than willing to oblige if it would need to be relicensed accordingly), but with a few points:

- The hexagon and "eagle" is an Americocentric design, with the hexagon partially being present as a reference to Node.js (on which the Passport library this was originally drawn for ran). For use as a standalone "password" icon, I've been meaning to replace that part of the icon with a shield or crest, to be more generally applicable internationally (most country's passports feature crests on the cover), and to distinguish from the original Passport.js use of the image. I'd want to finish that first.
- That image is an icon for *passports in general*, and as such would be more applicable to #3695 than this issue. This issue is for an icon that I would describe, for applicability in broader contexts, as ["headshot"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_shot).
- While the icon is visually *distinct* at small resolutions, I'm not sure it's *recognizable* at the angle I drew it at, and it's certainly not as easily hintable to the pixel grid. If including it in an icon font like this, it might make more sense to rotate it (though, when originally drawing it, I ran into a lot of trouble trying to make it adequately fill a square character space when aligned straight - not sure if that's really a priority, though).

Agreed, for file formats which specify an encoding (MDV), the only option is to cast the values in the arrays into the appropriate type after attempting to find reasonable scale/offset parameters if these are not provided by the user.  

For file types which do allow for a choice of encoding the question of what to do becomes more complicated.  I believe that with the [compression of variables available with NetCDF4](https://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/docs/netcdf/NetCDF_002d4-Format.html) there is little benefit to encoding values into integer types to save space while maintaining the original data precision, that is not "destroying data".  In addition when a loss of precision is acceptable there are more efficient manners of reducing the size of data than encoding data into other data types.

For example many radar moments in Sigmet files are stored with 16-bit of precision but Py-ART decodes these into a more usable 32-bit floating point values.  When these are written to uncompressed NetCDF files (for example NetCDF3 format which does not support compression) the file size are typically over two times the size of the original file.  When compression is turned on (the default for NETCDF4 files written with Py-ARTy), the file sizes are significantly reduced although still typically larger than the original raw size.  

The Cf/Radial files size can be further reduced by converting the field variables to integer values, but this nearly always is a lossy processed which reduces the precision of the data.  I consider an implicit 
loss of precision to be highly undesirable in scientific software and something that should be avoided.  Explicitly reducing the precision when a user requests such an operation is acceptable but the documentation should want about the consequences.   

Another other  when serializing data using the netcdf4-python library, is to use the[**least_significant_digit** parameter](http://unidata.github.io/netcdf4-python/netCDF4.Dataset-class.html#createVariable).  This truncates the values in the array allowing for more efficient compression which can results in reductions in the file size similar to or better than those possible by encoding the values in a different format.  Py-ART supports this type of truncation of data on a per variable basis by setting the **least_significant_digit** key in a field or variable dictionary (see [line 634 in cfradial.py](https://github.com/ARM-DOE/pyart/blob/4f566fc22338a5d44f392565a31da631e136fdce/pyart/io/cfradial.py#L634)).  This _feature_ is not well documented  which should be address.  In addition there are other options passed to the netcdf4-python library that can effect file size that should be exposed as parameters in the `write_cfradial` function.

@stuhood no. I'll close with #5073. That said, this is fully useable as long as the following is done in `pants.ini`:
```ini
[test.junit]
chroot: true # May require fixing test targets missing `Files` target dependencies for loose files (files read using jvm filesystem apis vs as resources).
batch_size: 1 # Equivalent of --no-test-pytest-fast, ie: run 1 test target at a time to provide fine grained cache hits

[test.pytest]
chroot: True # May require fixing test targets missing `Files` target dependencies for loose files (files read using python filesystem apis vs as pkgutil resources).
fast: False # Equivalent of --test-junit-batch-size=1, ie: run 1 test target at a time to provide fine grained cache hits

[cache.test.pytest]
read_from: ...
write_to: ...

[cache.test.junit]
read_from: ...
write_to: ...
```

In Django REST Framework's implementation, there is no way to expire a token by default. It is persistent, meaning that every time you log in, you are given the same auth token that was generated for you on the first registration/login. See http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/authentication/#tokenauthentication

If we are to add token expiration features, we should follow https://developers.facebook.com/blog/post/2011/05/13/how-to--handle-expired-access-tokens/ as there are a few cases where we need to expire the token (such as when we change the user's password).
This is not something we'll be able to get to for the LTS release (#4776) and the architecture in deis v2 has dramatically changed such that this is no longer relevant so I'm closing this.

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/logspout/logspout.go#L81

The buffer size set here is actually larger than the max UDP packet size-- the practical limit for which is 65,507 bytes (65,535 bytes minus 8 bytes of UDP headers and 20 bytes of IP headers).  No matter how big that buffer is set, a message larger than 65,507 bytes can not be sent in a single packet.

> either there should be an option to split lines over multiple UDP packets

UDP packets are by definition not related or ordered.  This would have to be implemented as an application-layer protocol.

> although that would make the streaming server/processes too complex

Exactly.  Beyond being complex, it would also be a ton of throwaway work since v2 doesn't use logspout.  Also, while we _could_ make the logger understand that custom app-layer protocol, other alternative log destinations not under our control (e.g. Papertrail) never would.

So this isn't an option.

> or just ignore lines above a (configurable) parameter and not sent them through, and as a result also not crash

fwiw, this is fixed in upstream logspout's master branch using a strategy something like that, but that hasn't made its way into a release yet and the pending release is a major one with many breaking changes.  Deis LTS won't be upgrading as it's too risky.

What I would propose be done here is that the code change such that the formatted string to be written (which includes the log message headers added by Deis for date/time and app, etc.) be prepared in advance (not on the fly).  That should be converted to bytes (to account for the possibility of non UTF-8 chars that consume more than a single byte) and the length of that byte slice should be compared to 65,507.  If it is any bigger, it should be trimmed down to 65,504 bytes and then have the bytes for "..." appended to the end.

This can be sent using `udpconn.Write(dataBytes)` and must _not_ be sent using `fmt.Fprint` since the latter is for writing strings; _not_ bytes.

I will gladly PR this is truncating in this fashion seems reasonable.  @bacongobbler thoughts?

fwiw, I think the TCP path through the code should receive the same treatment, but for different reasons.  Writing more bytes than can fit in a single packet is just fine when it comes to TCP, but on the logger end, there is still a simple assumption made that there is one log message per packet.

Just educated guessing here. 

The libgcc package is present when things don't work, and absent when they do. The libgcc package is a very old version (4.8.5). You probably need a libgcc that is closer to the compiler you're using. Ideally the same or newer version. 

Long term, we are updating our toolchain. Short term, there is a newer libgcc that might work - should be version 5.2.

By building with such a new gcc, though, you are imposing very rigid constraints on where your package will run. Even though your package works on your system, if you send it to someone with an older system libstdc++, it will not work. This is the idea behind the manylinux docker image and the conda_builder_linux docker image that never really caught on. I strongly recommend that you take steps to ensure wider compatibility.

I would strongly recommend *against* just generating errors. While we do do that for table, it has also caused problems - especially when combined with the ability to set default output (which essentially made some commands unusable by default if they were not supported by the output format).

Our output formatters should handle all commands that return data in our recommended formats. This specific command does not follow those guidelines (which is not surprising since it predates this particular recommendation :))

The alternative is to effectively require the user to specify which columns to show (in other word, you have to specify --query to project the exact columns that you want to see). There are other products that have solved this problem in this exact way.

Can't believe it's been nearly 3 years with no movement to supporting private PKIs.

The solutions suggested above all seem to expect you to own the code. That's not always the case and it's a bit sad that projects like `requests` have to resort to handling it separately. Trusting internal PKI is hard enough as it is, it would be great if `certifi` could provide a consistent interface around this.

I understand the point of this project is simply to provide the Mozilla root certs and creating a new project that provides the ability to add private CAs make sense. However, getting projects that already use `certifi` to now use a new module seems like a big ask, especially when this project could naturally evolve in that direction.

The reality is `requests` is such a popular project that unless `requests` switches to this a new wrapper module, I can't imagine others would follow quickly or easily. Given this project's tagline is `Trust Database for Humans`, I was hoping for a bit more.

I am probably just introducing noise at this point, but it would be awesome if there truly was a `Trust Database for Humans` just as `requests` is `HTTP for Humans`.

For users with superuser access, at least on Linux, adding the option `local_lock=all` to the `/etc/fstab` file or `/sbin/mount` command line should provide relief by bypassing server-side locking and doing all locking on the client.  This should **ONLY** be done in situations where it's guaranteed with absolute certainty that only 1 client will ever write to that SQLite database at a time; otherwise, corruption **will** occur.

Another possible option is SQLite proxy locking; see https://github.com/aosm/SQLite/blob/master/Notes/ProxyLocking.txt for details.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

Yes, the new semantics of `grad` can be a bit confusing. We've always had precisely the same semantics in `elementwise_grad` so the confusion itself isn't new. I'll try to explain the behavior here.

Originally, `grad` was only for scalar-output functions. We recently generalized it to accept array-output functions. The new definition of grad is actually very simple: `grad(f)` applies the transpose of the Jacobian of `f` to a vector of ones. Under some circumstances we can interpret this as a form of broadcasting (but see terminology note below). With a one-argument function `f` we can consider four cases:
  1. `f(x)` is a scalar. In this case `grad(f)(x)` gives the usual gradient.
  2. `f(x)` is an array with the same shape as `x`, and the Jacobian of `f` is diagonal. In this case, left-multiplying the Jacobian by a vector of ones pulls out the diagonal as a vector. So each `grad(f)(x)[i]` represents the derivative of `f(x)[i]` with respect to `x[i]`, which is analogous to numpy's usual automatic mapping by which scalar functions are applied elementwise to arrays.
  3. `f(x)` is an array with each dimension either equal to 1 or equal to the corresponding dimension of x (e.g. `x.shape == (2,3,4)` and `f(x).shape == (2,1,4)`) and the elements of the Jacobian corresponding to different input and output indices are all zero, with the exception of indices for the singleton output dimensions. In this case we end up with automatic mapping over non-singleton dimensions of `f(x)` and regular gradients over singleton dimensions of `f(x)`. Note that case 3 (which actually subsumes 1 and 2) only represents a subset of the cases for which `x` and `f(x)` are numpy-broadcastable. Specifically, `x` can't have singleton dimensions that don't correspond to singletons in `f(x)`. 
  4. In all other cases (such as yours) `grad(f)(x)` doesn't have much interpretation besides "the vector-Jacobian product of a vector of ones" or, equivalently, "the gradient of `lambda x: sum(f(x))`". We should consider having this case raise an error rather than returning a head-scratching result.

@mattjj and @j-towns, we often describe the new behavior of `grad` as 'broadcasting'. Broadcasting (whether numpy or radio) refers to a one-to-many fanout. I'd rather describe the new behavior of `grad` as "automatic mapping". I wonder if changing our terminology might help avoid confusion. Thoughts?

Powerline uses locale to determine the output encoding. When you use daemon output encoding is determined only once at startup and using environment daemon was launched in (mainly because I do not know how to make functions from `locale` module use given environment without modifying `os.environ` (which is not an option because this is global)).

If launchd does not specify correct locale and powerline detects used encoding as e.g. ASCII (this is normal for C locale which is the default) then it will only ever return ASCII characters. In this case powerline theme is automatically set to `ascii` unless it is provided in configuration, but forcing `powerline` theme makes no sense because output will be in encoding specified by locale.

* Okay, I've implemented `scipy.optimize.newton` for the "fast but unreliable" way (10x faster), and I've kept `scipy.optimize.fminbound` for the the "slow but reliable" way (2x faster).
* I also added the `i_x` and `i_xx` terms
* since both fast and slow use `bishop88` for curve points, then we can't compare it to `pvsystem.singlediode` since `bishop88` uses diode voltage as input, so the output cell voltages won't be the same for comparison
* since we can't compare them anyway, I'm in favor of sticking with the log-space voltages that are more tightly spaced near `v_oc` - unless everyone is against this

IMHO it doesn't make sense to use equal spacing since the points near `i_sc` will be on a nearly straight line, so you don't need that many points to define that region, but as the IV curve approaches `v_mp` it curves in a non-linear way, and then very close to `v_oc` it becomes very strongly sensitive to voltage so you would want the maximum number of points there.

It will be good to address the leaks too, but it is probably benign because the leaked references are garbage collected when the ast managers go out of scope, which they do in the par_or tactic.
They are most likely due to the fact that par_or invokes cancellation on running threads. 
The running threads may have created an untracked reference at the point where they 
react to cancellation (and throw an exception). 
We are not set up well to run repros with multi-threaded uses of Z3. The logging infrastructure only works for sequential uses of Z3. So a repro that exercises par_or would need to be a self-contained program. It may be possible to create those: store the current formula in an smt2 file, then in the repro parse that formula and start the tactic you have prepared. Given that threads are timing sensitive it may be useful to run this process in a loop.
I know this is a lot to ask for, but this is the simplest approach I can think of at this point.

First of all, I didn't see a Unicode string or a Unicode printing function declared in `display.h`. It seems that everything is a char array. There is no such thing called "Unicode string support" in binary analysis. All strings, Unicode or ANSI, are stored as bytes, and are accessed by instructions in similar ways. It's possible that there is a bug in our `printf` implementation that causes `SimMemoryLimitError` being raised when a very long string is fed to it, or when a character outside 0-127 is used as input. It'll be great if you can give us the backtrace of the exception: `errored_path.retry()` should give you the exception backtrace if you are running it in IPython.

I don't know what's wrong with the use_sim_procedures=False approach, but the strcpy simprocedure is subject to the limits on symbolic data we impose to make analysis tractable, specifically `state.libc.buf_symbolic_bytes`. The default value of this parameter is 60, so the internal "strlen" in the strcpy call only reports that you should copy at most 60 bytes. Try changing this and see what happens.

Configuring the number of workers is the easiest way to limit the amount of memory used since you will only have so many workers working at a single moment. As for configuring dask, right after the imports in your script do:

```python
import dask
from multiprocessing.pool import ThreadPool
with dask.config.set(pool=ThreadPool(4)):
    # all of your other satpy-based code goes in this block of code including the .show call
```

SatPy is already attempting to minimize the amount of data being processed when you resampled the data. There is another method but it currently has a bug so I'm not going to suggest it right now.

Lastly, you can configure the "chunks" that dask creates from the data arrays. Each chunk is processed separately on an individual dask worker when possible. You can configure this right now by specifying an environment variable although we hope to make it easier in the future. You can set `PYTROLL_CHUNK_SIZE` to a value between 1024 and 4096 (default) on the command line before running your script. For bash that would be:

```bash
export PYTROLL_CHUNK_SIZE=2048
python your_script.py
```

Lastly...again, you could also provide `cache_dir='/path/where/to/put/cache/files/'` to your `resample` call. This won't fix your memory errors but it will make processing of the same geostationary area much faster on future executions.

All DIRAC API calls should return a dictionary created by either [S_OK](http://diracgrid.org/files/docs/CodeDocumentation/Core/Utilities/ReturnValues.html#DIRAC.Core.Utilities.ReturnValues.S_OK) or [S_ERROR](http://diracgrid.org/files/docs/CodeDocumentation/Core/Utilities/ReturnValues.html#DIRAC.Core.Utilities.ReturnValues.S_ERROR). These will all have the key `'OK'` which maps to a bool. We should make sure that we are always checking this in `DiracCommands.py` with something like:
```python
result = deleteJob(257324)
if not result['OK']:
    raise GangaDiracError(result['Message'], result['Errno'])
...
carry on as usual
```
We then have to make sure that `execute()` propagates these exceptions correctly and then make sure that any call to execute is wrapped in a `try`/`except` at some level to make sure that these errors never make it to the user.

To recap, the goal with `ssh-agent` support is to allow the keys to be unlocked prior to running pulp smash and letting paramiko use the unlocked key. I briefly looked at the [paramiko docs on ssh agent integration](http://docs.paramiko.org/en/1.16/api/agent.html) and it seems that we would need explicit configuration to use `ssh-agent`. The feature part of `ssh-agent` integration is that a user's already unlocked key can be used. That's a nice to have, but optional as I see it. The important thing is that the key can be encrypted until paramiko goes to use it as specified from `~/.ssh/config`. Effectively when pulp smash is run if the key is encrypted the user will be prompted to have it unlocked. I think this will allow for secure configurations, even though it wouldn't work w/ `ssh-agent`.

  response = requests.post(url, data=payload, headers=myheaders, auth=(un, pw))

As an aside, I'd argue that this is a bug in `pip`. If we can get a fix for this released upstream, we could update `pipstrap` again to upgrade people to a new enough version to avoid the problem.

The reason to recreate the venv is to remove any packages we used to depend on but no longer do. Python being the dynamic language it is, the mere presence of an old package can occasionally affect the behavior of the program in a hard-to-debug way.

1. One easy quick fix (but with the consequence of a steady drip-drip of weird bug reports as old packages fester) is to stop recreating the venv.
2. The harder, more correct approach is to find a way to separate plugin-installed packages from the ones certbot itself needs. `pip install --target` (to install certbot's packages into a different folder) might be a part of this, but there are corner cases such as packages that both certbot and a third-party plugin need: where do they end up, and what if different versions cohabit in the same interpreter?
3. A third approach would be to come up with some less stateful way of installing third-party plugins, instead of just pip installing them. Then we could still recreate the venv but then, afterward, also automatically install the desired third-party plugs on top.

I think we'd be getting into some Real Crazy Stuff in 2. 3 would entail some nontrivial design work, user behavior changes, and troublesome corner cases if, say, we decided to express third-party plugins in terms of their PyPI package names but a private, unpublished plugin is needed. 1 is probably the way to go. It should be rare that a stale venv actually affects anything, and we can always add a flag (or a manual instruction) to start from a fresh venv as a troubleshooting step.

I'm sorry to chime in a bit late, but I think the solution here is a bit confused. The right way to solve this issue would really have been to use the `s*` specifier, so that many input types are accepted automatically (such memoryviews, bytearrays but also Numpy arrays).

Also, having to call PyBuffer_Release() at the end is a feature: it ensures that the input doesn't get mutated while you are acting on it (think what happens if one threads starts to lz4-compress a bytearray and the other thread reallocates the bytearray when shrinking it).

Also, I don't think returning a bytearray, while not detrimental, does not make particular sense. bytearrays are useful if you want to mutate the data; otherwise there's not much use to them. Also, some APIs only accept bytes objects (such as lz4 did :-)), so it's generally better to return bytes objects.

We made some performance measurements, and the main issue is this:
* For filtering by object-id (a property that is not filtered on the server side), the "List {Resource}s" operation is followed by a loop of "Get {Resource} Properties" operations, in order to get the object-id property, because it is not in the List result. That burns quite some time.
* This could be improved in either of two ways:
  - Add object-id to the properties returned by the List operations. We are discussing this internally.
  - Add a id-to-URI cache in the zhmcclient. This could be an extension to the existing name-to-URI cache.

ok.ru is indeed a sort of Russian Facebook (the other one being vk.com). Russians are using it for camshows and what-not (look at their [top page](https://ok.ru/live)) so it's no more inherently illegal than vaughnlive.tv or any other streaming network. Here is a plugin that does the job for live videos, feel free to add it or not:
```
import urllib3
import re

from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.plugin.api import http, validate
from streamlink.plugin.api import useragents
from streamlink.stream import HLSStream
from streamlink.utils import parse_json
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

class OK_live(Plugin):
    """
    Support for ok.ru live stream: http://www.ok.ru/live/
    """
    url_re = re.compile(r"https?://ok.ru/live/\d+")

    vod_hls_schema  = validate.Schema({u"hlsMasterPlaylistUrl": validate.url()},
                      validate.transform(lambda x: x["hlsMasterPlaylistUrl"]))
    vod_meta_schema = validate.Schema({u"flashvars": {u"metadata": validate.text}},
                      validate.transform(lambda x: x["flashvars"]["metadata"]))

    @classmethod
    def can_handle_url(cls, url):
        return cls.url_re.match(url) is not None

    def _get_streams(self):
        headers = {
            'User-Agent': useragents.CHROME,
            'Referer': self.url
        }

        soup = BeautifulSoup(http.get(self.url, headers=headers).text, 'html.parser')
        meta = parse_json(soup.find('div',attrs={'data-module':'OKVideo'})['data-options'], schema=self.vod_meta_schema)
        hls  = parse_json(meta, schema=self.vod_hls_schema)
        return HLSStream.parse_variant_playlist(self.session, hls, headers=headers)


__plugin__ = OK_live
```
Note: requires BeautifulSoup4 because really, parsing HTML with regular expressions?

@minrk, removing cross validation makes sense. My last question then, is whether we should validate defaults at all. As above, I suggested that going halfway with the validation simply makes things more confusing. However, to give users the ability to enforce validation when they need to, we can provide a bunch to default generators with the keys:

+ *name*: the name of the trait
+ *trait*: the associated trait
+ *default*: the "static" default generated by the trait's internal mechanisms.

IMO, because this makes default validation the responsibility of the user, it strikes a nice balance between the needs of those trying to validate, and the problems that can arise when they do.
